lesson: Set an explicit timeout on every outbound call (network, DB, lock); an unbounded wait turns one slow dependency into total thread-pool exhaustion.
Emit structured JSON logs with a correlation/request ID field propagated through every hop, header, and async job so one request's path is reconstructable from logs alone.
Implement a circuit breaker with closed/open/half-open states that trips after N consecutive failures and short-circuits calls during a cooldown, letting the dependency recover.
Isolate each downstream dependency into its own bulkhead (dedicated thread pool or connection pool) so one slow dependency can't starve capacity needed by unrelated requests.
Separate metrics (cheap aggregated time series for dashboards and alerts) from traces (expensive per-request causal spans for root-causing one slow request) - don't conflate them.
Apply backpressure with bounded queues and explicit load-shedding (fast 503/429) instead of unbounded buffering, which only delays an OOM crash further downstream.
Degrade gracefully: serve a cached, stale, or default response when a non-critical dependency times out instead of failing the entire request for one slow feature.
Cap metric label cardinality - never use raw user IDs, full URLs, or UUIDs as tags - high-cardinality labels blow up time-series storage and crash metrics backends.
Alert on user-facing symptoms (error-rate or latency-percentile SLO burn) not causes (CPU%, disk%); cause-based alerts fire on noise without confirming real user impact.
